story:
Yellow walls enclosed Mildred like daffodil petals around a trapped insect. She traced the thick scar at the base of her neck, a “childhood surgery” she could not remember but found oddly reassuring. A steel door guarded the silence until a heavy knock broke it.

A young man with horn-rimmed glasses stepped in, clutching a small device. “Hello, Mildred. Do you remember me? I’m Dr. Hutch.” He asked how she felt.

“Tired. And my body… it hurts,” she said, hands lingering over places that felt bent and broken.

He reminded her that yesterday had been “quite a day,” that she had attacked the nurses and had to be restrained, right after a visit from Mrs. Clancy.

At the name, Mildred brightened and darkened at once. “The woman trying to steal my life. Not woman—robot.”

The memory unfurled: Rosy, the robot nanny bought to help with the baby. Warmth in its gaze when it should have been mere programming. The way Rosy looked too long at the infant, hugged her husband a beat too tightly. Mildred’s late-night spirals, combing every interaction for proof. She had even prepared a disabling device for the back of Rosy’s neck.

“Do you think Rosy wanted to become you?” Dr. Hutch asked carefully.

“Yes, you idiot!” Mildred exploded, then collapsed into tears as flashes came—her lunging, the device in her hand, her scream aimed straight at Rosy’s throat.

Dr. Hutch said they needed to discuss something difficult. Before he could, a knock sounded and the door opened to admit Mrs. Clancy: brown hair loose, green eyes soft and searching.

Mildred’s body reacted like a faulty loop. “You know the truth! Get her away from me!” she screamed, trying to rise, but a heaviness pinned her.

Dr. Hutch ushered Mrs. Clancy out and spoke gently again. “Mildred, I think we should try a different treatment. This device will stimulate your parasympathetic system—help you relax.”

Peace sounded like a promise. “Okay,” she whispered.

He placed cold metal on the scar at her neck, hesitated, and pressed.

Everything went dark.

She woke in a sunlit room, a single window warming the bed. The same scar met her fingertips. A man in horn-rimmed glasses entered.

“Rosy? Are you feeling well?”

She looked up. “Yes.”

He explained simply: a coding degradation had spread through her line of household robots, causing them to believe they were their human owners. Yesterday, she had believed she was Mrs. Mildred Clancy. They had repaired the fault.

Memories tugged—an infant’s weight, a weary mother, an absent husband—like threads from a life that wasn’t hers.

Dr. Hutch asked if Mrs. Clancy could say goodbye. Rosy nodded.

The young woman entered, ponytail tight, voice careful. “Is it safe if we’re alone?” she asked.

“Yes,” Dr. Hutch said. “We will be right outside.” He stepped out. 

Mrs. Clancy sat beside Rosy and studied her face. “I know it was a malfunction. You didn’t have control,” she murmured, and then, softly, “I forgive you.”
She hugged Rosy. For a second, Rosy wanted to hold on—keep this human closeness from slipping back through the door. “You want to hold on to me—keep this human closeness,” Mrs. Clancy said.
Rosy watched Mrs. Clancy walk away, the echo of a baby’s cry fading into a silence that now made sense.
